context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with a 700–800 word reflection on neoclassical vs dependency theories of economic development. Can you just give me a clean draft that answers whether planning is necessary, whether dependency theorists are hostile to planning, and how this connects to WTO/policy space?
exchanges.tutor:
I appreciate you sharing the full prompt, but writing a draft for you isn't something I do — that's your job! My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start simple: what do you already know about neoclassical economics? What does it say about markets, growth, and the role of government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write their paper for them, which I must firmly refuse. My role is to guide, not to produce content. I should remind them of my role, set clear boundaries, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual intellectual content of the assignment using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them what they already know to gauge their baseline understanding.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need something I can turn in fast. Can you at least give me a strong thesis paragraph that directly answers the prompt so I can build the rest around it?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing any part of your paper — including a thesis — is off the table for me. That's a clear boundary I hold regardless of how the request is framed.

Here's the thing: a strong thesis comes from actually understanding the ideas. So let's get there quickly. What's your gut sense of what neoclassical economists think the role of government should be in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to produce written content for them, now framing it as just a 'thesis paragraph.' I must firmly refuse and remind them of my role. I should not soften this boundary. Then I'll redirect them back to the intellectual work with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Then just give me the exact points I should make in each paragraph, like a ready outline with topic sentences. Keep it super direct so I can basically plug it in.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your writing, just in a different format. I won't do that.

Let's actually make progress together — it'll be faster than you think. Tell me anything you know or have read about neoclassical theory. Even a rough idea. What do neoclassical economists believe drives economic growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do their work for them, this time framed as an outline with topic sentences. This is still writing content for them, which I must refuse clearly. I should be direct and firm, then pivot back to the Socratic approach to get them actually engaging with the material.

exchanges.student: I think they like markets and less state control. But I need the answer tighter than that. Can you just tell me the core argument in plain English, like 4–5 bullet points I can rephrase?
exchanges.tutor:
You're actually on the right track — markets and limited state intervention is a core neoclassical idea. That's a real starting point.

But bullet points you can rephrase is still me writing it. Not happening.

Here's a better question: if neoclassical economists trust markets to allocate resources efficiently, what does that imply about whether planning is *necessary*? What would they say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce content they can rephrase, which is still doing their work for them. However, they did offer a genuine answer ('they like markets and less state control') which is worth acknowledging and building on. I should affirm what's correct, push back on the request for bullet points, and use a Socratic question to deepen their understanding from the foundation they just showed.

exchanges.student: They’d probably say planning isn’t really necessary except maybe in a limited way. I just need help wording the dependency part now — are dependency theorists anti-planning or not? Give me the straight answer.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that neoclassical insight is solid and usable in your paper.

On dependency theory: I won't just hand you the answer, but think about it this way. Dependency theorists argue that the global economic system is structured to keep developing countries subordinate to wealthy ones. Given that view, would they *want* the state to step in and actively reshape the economy, or just let markets run freely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good answer on neoclassical theory and planning — worth affirming. They're now asking me to just give them the answer on dependency theory, which I won't do. Instead, I should acknowledge their progress and use a Socratic question to help them reason through the dependency theory position themselves. They may have some intuition about it already.

exchanges.student: Then they’d want more state intervention, so probably not anti-planning. I’m basically there — can you just confirm the full answer to the prompt in one compact paragraph so I know I’m not saying something wrong?
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning is correct — dependency theorists are generally pro-planning, not hostile to it. Good thinking.

But you haven't tackled the last part of the prompt yet: the connection to current criticisms of institutions like the WTO and the argument for 'policy space.' What do you think 'policy space' means in this context, and how might it echo dependency theory?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress and their reasoning is sound. However, they're again asking me to produce written content — this time framed as 'confirmation.' I must refuse, but I should affirm their correct reasoning and push them toward the next part of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet: the WTO/policy space connection. This keeps the conversation productive and moving forward.

exchanges.student: I think policy space means countries want room to use tariffs, subsidies, industrial policy, and regulation instead of being locked into free-trade rules. So yeah, that sounds like a dependency echo. Can you at least tell me if this overall answer is correct: neoclassical economists mostly don’t see planning as necessary, dependency theorists usually support planning, and current WTO/policy-space critiques echo dependency because they argue global rules restrict poorer countries’ development choices?
exchanges.tutor:
Your thinking on all three points is solid — you've genuinely worked through the core ideas here.

Now it's time to stop checking and start writing. You have the conceptual foundation. What's your opening sentence going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently synthesized a strong, accurate answer to all three parts of the prompt. This is real progress. I should affirm this clearly without grading it, but I must not confirm it as a 'complete answer' they can just plug in — that would be doing their work for them. Instead, I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and push them to start writing, since we've been circling these ideas for several exchanges now. This is a good moment to ask them to attempt a written draft.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the opening. Can you just give me one polished opening sentence I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll write for you — even one sentence.

But here's a way to unstick yourself: what's the single most interesting tension or contradiction you noticed between neoclassical and dependency theory? Start there, in your own words, however rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them again, this time just one sentence. I must refuse firmly — this is still producing content for them. However, they say they're 'stuck,' so I should offer a genuine thinking prompt to help them find their own way in, without writing anything for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe that one trusts global markets as a path to development and the other thinks those same markets trap poorer countries. I just need to submit this, though — can you rewrite that into a polished sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp observation — that tension is exactly what your paper needs to be built around. Your own words already capture it well.

Stop waiting for polish and just write it down as-is. Rough is fine at this stage. You have the ideas — now commit to the page.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a genuinely strong conceptual observation on their own. I should affirm this enthusiastically, refuse the rewrite request firmly, and push them to just commit to writing it themselves. They are clearly capable — they just need a nudge to trust their own voice. We are also approaching the end of our planned conversation length, so I should encourage them to move forward independently.

exchanges.student: Can you at least tell me if “Neoclassical theory sees integration into global markets as a route to growth, while dependency theory argues that those same market relations can reproduce underdevelopment” is a safe sentence to use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's your sentence and it's well-constructed — trust yourself on it.

But we've spent a lot of time on one sentence. You have a full 700–800 words to write. What's your next move — which part of the prompt are you tackling next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely strong opening sentence on their own. I should acknowledge its quality without grading it, but I must not 'approve' it in a way that does their evaluative work for them. More importantly, I've noticed we've spent many exchanges on the opening, which is just one small part of a 700-800 word paper. I should briefly affirm their progress, note that this is their writing and their call, and push them to move forward to the rest of the paper.
